problem_statement.Title: Assertion rewrite crashes when module's first top-level expression is a non-string constant (e.g. a number)
problem_statement.Description: pytest's assertion rewriter raises TypeError during collection when the first top-level expression in a test module is a non-string ast.Constant (for example a numeric literal). The rewriting logic treats that first constant as a module docstring and passes it to a function that expects a string, causing a TypeError ('argument of type "int" is not iterable').
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) AssertionRewriter.run() (src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py) detects a module docstring by treating the first top-level ast.Expr whose value is an ast.Constant as the module docstring without verifying the Constant's .value type. Location: AssertionRewriter.run() in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py (lines ~668–683). 2) When that first ast.Constant is used, run() assigns doc = item.value.value and immediately calls is_rewrite_disabled(doc). Location: same as above (lines ~680–682). 3) AssertionRewriter.is_rewrite_disabled() assumes its argument is a str and performs a substring test: "PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE" in docstring, which raises TypeError when docstring is not a str (e.g., an int). Location: AssertionRewriter.is_rewrite_disabled() in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py (lines ~744–746). 4) The initial doc retrieval uses getattr(mod, "docstring", None), which does not guarantee the returned value is a str, so the code's downstream checks can receive non-str values. Location: AssertionRewriter.run() in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py (line ~668). Correct relationship: only values that are instances of str should be treated as module docstrings; the first top-level expression should be considered a docstring only if it is a string constant. Any non-string constant (e.g., numeric literal) must not be passed to the substring-checking function.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python file (test module) whose first top-level statement is a numeric literal, for example:
def test_example():
assert 1 == 2
Run pytest collecting that file (e.g. pytest -k test_example).
Observe the collection error with traceback ending in TypeError: argument of type 'int' is not iterable coming from _pytest/assertion/rewrite.py.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: pytest should collect the module without raising TypeError. The assertion-rewriting phase should examine a module's first top-level expression and consider it a docstring only if it is an actual string object; the 'PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE' sentinel should only be searched for in string docstrings.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During collection, pytest raises TypeError: argument of type 'int' is not iterable originating from AssertionRewriter.is_rewrite_disabled(), because a non-string ast.Constant (the first top-level expression, e.g. 123) was treated as the module docstring and passed to a function that does a substring test ("PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE" in docstring).

problem_statement.Title: pmxbot.logging loaded twice under different module names; Logger.store set on one copy but not visible to tests
problem_statement.Description: After switching to import-mode=importlib and PEP 420 namespace packages, running the tests shows core.initialize(config) sets pmxbot.logging.Logger.store on one module object, but tests raise AttributeError because the logging module / Logger class visible to the test is a different module object than the one modified by initialize.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) core.initialize mutates state on the pmxbot.logging module object (it sets Logger.store) but assumes the logging source will be imported exactly once under the canonical module name 'pmxbot.logging'. Location (as reported): core.initialize (caller: core.initialize(config)).

2) At test time the same source file is loaded under a different module name, producing a second module object. The test-visible module name observed in the failure context is tests.unit.test_commands.logging (Location: tests/unit/test_commands.py as reported). Both module objects define their own Logger class objects. The test's Logger does not have the .store attribute because that attribute was set on the other module's Logger object.

3) The project/test configuration change to import-mode=importlib and the use of PEP 420 namespace packages changes import resolution and can allow the same .py file to be bound to multiple module names (this configuration is reported by the user). This makes the single-import assumption in (1) unsafe in that environment.

4) Concrete Python-level relationship producing the failure: when the same file is imported twice under two different names, sys.modules contains two distinct module objects; each module defines distinct class objects. Mutating a class attribute (Logger.store) on one class object does not affect the distinct class object defined in the other module object. Evidence in the failing run: AttributeError on logging.Logger.store, and in the PDB session sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging returned False, indicating two different module objects refer to the same source file.
StepsToReproduce: Checkout the pmxbot tree at the reported state (pmxbot/pmxbot@7f189ad) and ensure project/test configuration uses import-mode=importlib and the PEP 420 namespace package layout described in the report.
Run the unit tests that exercise core.initialize and the logging facility, e.g.: run pytest for tests/unit/test_commands.py (or run the full test suite as in the report).

Observe the failure in TestCommands.setup_class when core.initialize(config) is called and the subsequent access logging.Logger.store.message(...) raises AttributeError: type object 'Logger' has no attribute 'store'.

Optionally, in a failing test run drop to a debugger and inspect: the logging module object imported by the test vs sys.modules['pmxbot.logging']; confirm they are distinct objects (report example: sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging -> False).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After core.initialize(config) runs, importing pmxbot.logging and accessing pmxbot.logging.Logger.store should succeed: the Logger class referenced by test code should have its .store attribute set and usable by callers.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Tests raise AttributeError when accessing logging.Logger.store because the test-visible logging module is a different module object (loaded under a different module name) than the one core.initialize mutated; the Logger class in the test-visible module has no .store attribute.

problem_statement.Title: Assertion rewriter prints generator object for assert all(<generator expr>) instead of itemized failure
problem_statement.Description: When an assertion uses all(...) or any(...) with a generator expression, pytest's assertion rewriter produces an unhelpful failure message that shows the generator object (e.g. <generator object ...>) rather than the evaluated items or which item/predicate failed. The problem is visible in test assertions using generator expressions but not when using an explicit for-loop or an explicit list comprehension.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) No specialized visitor for generator expressions: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter does not implement a visit_GeneratorExp (no visit_GeneratorExp is defined in the module). Because of this, generator expression AST nodes are handled by the generic expression path rather than a generator-specific visitor.

2) Call handling does not special-case builtins 'all'/'any': src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35 and ::visit_Call_legacy visit call.func and call.args generically (they iterate over call.args and call.keywords and build a new ast.Call). Neither method detects the pattern of a call to the builtin 'all' or 'any' whose single positional argument is an ast.GeneratorExp, so no special representation is produced for that case (both methods are present and must be considered: visit_Call_35 for Python 3.5+ and visit_Call_legacy for older versions).

3) Generic expression handling yields saferepr of the temporary: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter.generic_visit (with assign(), display(), explanation_param()) encodes the path visit(expr) -> res = self.assign(expr) -> self.explanation_param(self.display(res)). display() calls the module helper _saferepr which uses saferepr to produce the textual representation. For a generator expression this results in the generator object's repr (e.g. '<generator object ...>'), which is the immediate cause of the uninformative assertion message.

4) Same root pattern affects other lazy iterables: any expression that produces a lazy iterator-like object (for example, certain uses of map/filter or calls that return iterator objects) will follow the same generic path and thus will also have their repr shown (not their materialized items) unless handled specially in the rewriter. This is a parity-level observation — the code paths above are the ones that encode the assumption that leaving such expressions unchanged will yield a useful explanation.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file test_all_any.py with the following content:
def is_even(n):
return n % 2 == 0
def test_all_even():
even_stevens = list(range(1,100,2))
assert all(is_even(number) for number in even_stevens)
Run pytest on the file (pytest test_all_any.py) and observe the assertion failure output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When assert all(<predicate generator>) or assert any(<predicate generator>) fails, the failure message should show the evaluated items or indicate which item/predicate failed (for example, a list of boolean results or pinpoint the failing element), providing the same useful diagnostic detail as an equivalent explicit for-loop assertion.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For assert all(<generator expression>) the failure message shows the generator object repr (e.g. 'all(<generator object ...>)'), which is not helpful. Writing the same expression as a list comprehension shows a list of booleans, and writing an explicit for-loop yields a precise per-item assertion failure (the for-loop message is informative).

problem_statement.Title: pytest --fixtures / --fixtures-per-test do not display fixture scope
problem_statement.Description: When running pytest --fixtures or pytest --fixtures-per-test the printed fixture listings do not show each fixture's scope (session/package/module/class/function). The only way to learn a fixture's scope is to read docs or inspect source.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Presentation omission in fixture-listing routines: The functions that render fixture listings in src/_pytest/python.py build a funcargspec string for each fixture but never include the fixture's scope. In the per-test listing, write_fixture constructs funcargspec as either "{} -- {}".format(argname, bestrel) or argname (src/_pytest/python.py, lines around 1257-1266). In the global listing, _showfixtures_main constructs funcargspec as "%s -- %s" % (argname, bestrel) or argname (src/_pytest/python.py, lines around 1345-1349). These format strings do not reference fixture.scope.

2) Scope data exists but is unused by the printer: Fixture scope is stored on fixture objects created by the fixture machinery (FixtureDef.scope is assigned in FixtureDef.__init__, src/_pytest/fixtures.py, lines around 829-833) and scopenum is computed via scope2index. The canonical scope strings are defined in the fixtures module as: ["session", "package", "module", "class", "function"] (src/_pytest/fixtures.py, line defining scopes). The printing code reads FixtureDef instances (from the session's FixtureManager) but does not reference fixturedef.scope when building user-visible output.

3) Pseudo/other fixture-like objects contain scope as well: PseudoFixtureDef (used for direct parametrization) also exposes a scope attribute (src/_pytest/fixtures.py, PseudoFixtureDef declaration, lines around 42-46). Therefore the absence of scope in listings is a presentation-layer omission rather than a lack of scope information in the fixture model.
StepsToReproduce: Create or open a pytest project with at least one fixture (for example, a fixture defined in conftest.py or a test module).
Run: pytest --fixtures
Observe the list of available fixtures and note that the scope (function/class/module/package/session) is not shown next to fixture names.
Optionally run: pytest --fixtures-per-test and observe fixture lists for individual tests; scope is likewise not shown.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The fixture listing commands (pytest --fixtures and pytest --fixtures-per-test) should show each fixture's scope in the user-visible listing (for example: "my_fixture (module) -- path/to/file.py" in verbose mode, or "my_fixture (module)" in non-verbose mode).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Fixture listings omit the scope entirely and display only the fixture name (and, in verbose mode, a location) such as "my_fixture" or "my_fixture -- path/to/file.py" with no scope information.

problem_statement.Title: Default logging format omits logger name (DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT)
problem_statement.Description: The module-level DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT used by pytest's logging plugin only shows the base filename and line number (with fixed-width alignment) and does not include the logger name (%(name)s). As a result, log output is less informative for projects that use hierarchical logger names.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT constant in src/_pytest/logging.py is hardcoded to "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" and therefore omits the logger name (%(name)s). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the base filename and line number alone are sufficient identifiers for a log's origin. Location: src/_pytest/logging.py (DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT at line 18). Note: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT is used as the default for the --log-format option (add_option_ini at lines ~109-112) and as the default for --log-file-format (add_option_ini at lines ~154-157); LoggingPlugin constructs its logging.Formatter from the log_format option in LoggingPlugin.__init__ (lines ~396-399) and constructs the file formatter from the log_file_format (lines ~403-409). Also, in _setup_cli_logging, log_cli_format falls back to log_format when not set (line ~440), so the DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT value propagates to CLI live-logging when log_cli_format is unset.

2) The DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT embeds fixed-width/alignment tokens ("%(filename)-25s", "%(lineno)4d", "%(levelname)-8s") which encode a layout-focused assumption (column alignment and filename-as-primary-identifier). Those hardcoded widths are appropriate only under that specific layout assumption and conflict with the goal of surfacing the full logger name as the primary context. Location: src/_pytest/logging.py (DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT at line 18).

3) The ColoredLevelFormatter inspects the format string for a "%(levelname)...s" fragment (LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX = re.compile(r"%\(levelname\)([+-]?\d*s)") at line ~36). Colorization behavior relies on the presence of that fragment (for example, "%(levelname)-8s"). Any change to the default format should preserve the levelname token format if colored CLI output is expected to remain functional. Location: src/_pytest/logging.py (ColoredLevelFormatter.LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX and constructor usage).

4) A unit test (testing/logging/test_formatter.py) contains the old format string literal "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" and uses it as explicit input to test ColoredLevelFormatter. Because the test supplies the literal explicitly, it will not fail if the module-level DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT constant changes; however, it encodes an expectation of the previous layout that maintainers may want to review. Location: testing/logging/test_formatter.py (line where logfmt is defined).

Preferred/proposed value observed in investigation: "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s" (this is a diagnostic example of a format that includes the logger name; stating the current and the preferred value documents the incorrect hardcoded value).
StepsToReproduce: Have a project with logs emitted from different modules (so logger names differ).
Run pytest with default settings (do not set --log-format).
Observe log output lines produced by pytest (CLI or log file).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: By default, log lines include the logger name (module/package) so that emitted messages clearly show the module context (for example: "DEBUG    django.db.backends:utils.py:114 (0.000) SELECT ...").
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: By default, log lines show only the base filename and line number with fixed-width alignment (for example: "utils.py                   114 DEBUG    (0.000) SELECT ..."), omitting the logger name (%(name)s).

problem_statement.Title: str(ExceptionInfo) (pytest.raises context) shows only first line of exception message (truncated by ReprFileLocation)
problem_statement.Description: When using pytest.raises(...) as e the object bound to e is an ExceptionInfo. Calling str(e) prints a file:lineno plus only the first line of the exception text instead of the full multi-line exception message (whereas str(e.value) prints the full text). This is visible when raising exceptions whose messages contain newlines.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ExceptionInfo.__str__ (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:537-542) constructs a ReprFileLocation from the last traceback entry and self.exconly() and returns its string form. Concretely: it does

    entry = self.traceback[-1]
    loc = ReprFileLocation(entry.path, entry.lineno + 1, self.exconly())
    return str(loc)

   which wraps the (potentially multi-line) exconly() text in a ReprFileLocation for stringification.

2) ReprFileLocation.toterminal (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:950-955) enforces a single-line summary by truncating the message at the first newline. The truncation is implemented as:

    msg = self.message
    i = msg.find("\n")
    if i != -1:
        msg = msg[:i]

   This encodes the assumption that file:lineno messages should be a single-line summary suitable for editor parsing/clickable output.

3) ExceptionInfo.exconly (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:460-466) returns the full exception text via traceback.format_exception_only(...) and .rstrip(), so the multi-line content is available; it is the wrapping with ReprFileLocation (and its newline truncation) that causes the visible loss of lines when callers use str(ExceptionInfo).

4) The same wrapping/truncation pattern is also used elsewhere when building file:lineno summaries and reprcrash values: ExceptionInfo._getreprcrash (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:472-476) returns ReprFileLocation(path, lineno + 1, exconly(tryshort=True)) and FormattedExcinfo.repr_traceback_entry (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:681-686) constructs ReprFileLocation(path, entry.lineno + 1, message). Those locations therefore share the same assumption and are subject to the same first-newline truncation behavior.

5) Observable consequence: printing the ExceptionInfo object with str(excinfo) yields the file:lineno plus only the first line of the exception only (e.g. "<console>:3: LookupError: A"), whereas printing the actual exception instance (str(excinfo.value)) yields the full multi-line message ("A\nB\nC").
StepsToReproduce: Create an exception whose message contains newlines.

Example B (pytest.raises context):
with pytest.raises(LookupError) as e:
    raise LookupError("A\nB\nC")
print(str(e))
This prints only a file:lineno plus the first line of the message (e.g. "<console>:3: LookupError: A").
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When printing the object returned by pytest.raises (the ExceptionInfo), str(excinfo) should present the full exception message visible to the user (the same multi-line text returned by str(excinfo.value)), or at least should not silently drop lines such that str(excinfo) and str(excinfo.value) differ in the visible message content.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: str(excinfo) returns a file:lineno prefix followed by only the first line of the exception message (because ReprFileLocation truncates at the first '\n'), e.g. "<console>:3: LookupError: A". To see the full message the user must call str(excinfo.value).

hints_text:
> Any chance the behavior could be changed to eliminate this gotcha?

What do you suggest?

Proxying through to the exceptions `__str__`?
Hi @fiendish,

Indeed this is a bit confusing.

Currently `ExceptionInfo` objects (which is `pytest.raises` returns to the context manager) implements `__str__` like this:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/9f8b566ea976df3a3ea16f74b56dd6d4909b84ee/src/_pytest/_code/code.py#L537-L542

I don't see much use for this, I would rather it didn't implement `__str__` at all and let `__repr__` take over, which would show something like:

```
<ExceptionInfo LookupError tb=10>
```

Which makes it more obvious that this is not what the user intended with `str(e)` probably.

So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Thoughts?

Also, @fiendish which Python version are you using?
> So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Makes sense to me.


Python 3.7.3

My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.
> My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.

I understand, but I think it is better to be explicit here, because users might use `print(e)` to see what `e` is, assume it is the exception value, and then get confused later when it actually isn't (an `isinstance` check or accessing `e.args`).
+1 for deleting the current `__str__` implementation
-1 for proxying it to the underlying `e.value`

the `ExceptionInfo` object is not the exception and anything that makes it look more like the exception is just going to add to the confusion

problem_statement.Title: Pytest shows integer ordinals (e.g. 52) when diffing bytes because bytes are treated as sequences
problem_statement.Description: When pytest rewrites assertions comparing two bytes objects (for example: assert b'' == b'42'), the assertion message displays element-wise sequence information that reveals Python 3's bytes indexing semantics (indexing yields an int). This produces confusing output such as "first extra item: 52" where 52 is the ASCII code of '4', instead of a readable byte-string diff.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) assertrepr_compare (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py) treats only str as "text" (istext uses isinstance(x, str)). Because bytes are not considered text, they fall through to the sequence-comparison branch (issequence returns True for bytes since bytes is a Sequence but not str). Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, function assertrepr_compare (op == '==' dispatch and the 'not in' dispatch).

2) _compare_eq_sequence (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py) compares elements by indexing and formats messages using saferepr on individual elements (for example, it prints "first extra item: %s" using saferepr(right[len_left])). For bytes on Python 3, indexing yields an int (the byte ordinal), so saferepr prints that integer (e.g. '52') rather than a byte-character or a byte-string fragment. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, function _compare_eq_sequence.

3) saferepr (src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py) correctly represents the Python object it is given (for an int it displays '52'). The confusing output arises because saferepr is being given an integer produced by indexing bytes, not because saferepr is incorrect. Location: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py, SafeRepr utilities.

4) _diff_text (src/_pytest/assertion/util.py) already documents and implements support for bytes by converting them to readable text before diffing. However, assertrepr_compare's current dispatch does not route bytes==bytes comparisons to _diff_text, so the bytes-aware diff code is not used in that case. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, function _diff_text. Note: _diff_text contains trimming constants (e.g. a threshold of 42 characters used when skipping identical leading characters) which apply to the text form obtained after converting bytes and are not a cause of the confusing numeric output.

Summary (all findings equal weight):
- "Bytes are being represented by generic sequence element diffs" (encoded in assertrepr_compare via the issequence branch). Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, assertrepr_compare.
- "Only str objects are considered text and are diffed with _diff_text" (istext checks only for str). Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py, assertrepr_compare and related 'not in' handling.
- The practical effect: when both operands are bytes (or bytearray), the sequence-based element display indexes bytes yielding ints; saferepr renders those ints (e.g. 52), causing the confusing message.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file test_bytes_diff.py with the following content:
def test_b():
assert b'' == b'42'
Run pytest on that file and observe the assertion message.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When two bytes objects are compared and the assertion fails, pytest should present a human-readable byte-string diff (similar in clarity to how it diffs str) showing the differing bytes or a whole-bytes diff, not integer ordinals. For example, the output should show the readable bytes representation b'42' (or a + b'42' style diff) rather than the ASCII ordinal 52.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest displays sequence-style diffs for bytes where element indexing yields ints, producing messages like "Right contains more items, first extra item: 52" (52 is the ordinal for '4'), which is confusing because it shows an integer instead of a readable byte/byte-string representation.

hints_text: hmmm yes, this ~kinda makes sense as `bytes` objects are sequences of integers -- we should maybe just omit the "contains more items" messaging for bytes objects?

problem_statement.Title: pytest JUnit XML <testsuite> missing timestamp and hostname attributes
problem_statement.Description: When pytest generates a JUnit XML report via the junitxml reporter, the top-level <testsuite> element does not include the standard timestamp and hostname attributes (for example: timestamp="2019-04-22T10:32:27" hostname="host.local").
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/junitxml.py — LogXML.pytest_sessionfinish: when the <testsuite> element is constructed the code calls Junit.testsuite(...) with only the attributes name, errors, failures, skipped, tests and time (see the Junit.testsuite call in src/_pytest/junitxml.py around lines 660–669). The testsuite construction does not include timestamp or hostname keyword arguments, so those attributes are never emitted.

2) src/_pytest/junitxml.py — LogXML.pytest_sessionstart/pytest_sessionfinish: pytest records self.suite_start_time = time.time() at session start (line 641) and uses it to compute the testsuite duration (suite_time_delta used for the time attribute, lines 648–649). However, that recorded start time is not used to produce a textual testsuite timestamp attribute (i.e. there is no attribute like timestamp="..." generated from suite_start_time), demonstrating the absence of a testsuite timestamp despite the start time being available.

3) testing/test_junitxml.py — junit XML tests validate existing testsuite attributes (name, errors, failures, skipped, tests, time) but do not assert the presence of testsuite timestamp or hostname. Example: tests use node.assert_attr(name="pytest", errors=..., failures=..., skipped=..., tests=...) (e.g. testing/test_junitxml.py lines ~111–115). Because tests do not check for timestamp/hostname presence, there is no test preventing the current omission from regressing.

4) Repository-wide search: there are no other occurrences of 'hostname' or 'gethostname' producing a testsuite hostname anywhere in the repository; 'suite_start_time' appears only in src/_pytest/junitxml.py. This indicates the omission is localized to the junitxml writer and not implemented elsewhere.

5) Expected attribute relationships/formats (diagnostic expectations based on common JUnit examples and the original report): timestamp is expected to be derived from the suite start time and formatted in an ISO-like form "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S" (example: "2019-04-22T10:32:27"); hostname is expected to be the machine hostname, typically obtained via socket.gethostname() (example: "Anass-MacBook-Pro.local"). These describe the missing attribute values and formats (they are diagnostics of what is absent, not implementation instructions).
StepsToReproduce: Create or use a project with at least one pytest test.
Run pytest with the JUnit XML output option, e.g. pytest --junitxml=report.xml
Open the generated report.xml and inspect the top-level <testsuite> element; observe that timestamp and hostname attributes are not present.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element includes timestamp and hostname attributes in addition to name, tests, errors, failures, skipped and time. Example: <testsuite name="..." tests="..." errors="..." failures="..." skipped="..." timestamp="YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS" hostname="<machine-hostname>" time="...">
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element contains attributes name, tests, errors, failures, skipped and time but does not contain timestamp or hostname (those attributes are missing from the testsuite tag).

problem_statement.Title: pytest --collect-only has no one-character (short) CLI alias
problem_statement.Description: The --collect-only option is only registered as long-form flags and has no one-character shortcut; users must type the full long option to request collection-only mode.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Option registered only with long names: in pytest_addoption (src/_pytest/main.py) the collect-mode option is registered as group.addoption("--collectonly", "--collect-only", action="store_true", help=...). No single-character optstring is passed at registration (location: src/_pytest/main.py:108-114).

2) Parser destination derived from first long option: the argument machinery sets the destination (dest) from the first long option when dest is not supplied (dest = self._long_opts[0][2:].replace('-', '_')). Because the first long option in the registration is "--collectonly" the resulting config attribute is config.option.collectonly; adding a short alias does not change the destination so long as that first long option remains present (location: src/_pytest/config/argparsing.py:224-227).

3) Short-option reservation rule in option-group API: OptionGroup.addoption and OptionGroup._addoption differ in whether lowercase single-letter short options are permitted. OptionGroup.addoption calls _addoption_instance with shortupper=False and the implementation raises ValueError if any short option is a lowercase single-letter (i.e. opt[1].islower()). OptionGroup._addoption calls _addoption_instance with shortupper=True which allows lowercase single-letter shorts. This explains why some code paths use group._addoption for registering single-letter shortcuts while other addoption calls only permit (or expect) long-option forms (location: src/_pytest/config/argparsing.py:302-326).

4) Collision risk with existing single-letter options: many one-character flags are already registered across pytest (examples: "-x" and "-c" in src/_pytest/main.py, "-s" in src/_pytest/capture.py, "-h", "-p", "-o" in src/_pytest/helpconfig.py, "-k", "-m" in src/_pytest/mark/__init__.py, "-v", "-q", "-r", "-l" in src/_pytest/terminal.py). Choosing a lowercase letter for a new one-character alias risks collision with these existing short options; uppercase single-letter options are less common and are treated differently by OptionGroup.addoption/_addoption semantics (various registrations cited above).

5) Concrete encoding of the absence: the immediate cause is the literal optnames passed to addoption: the registration lists only the two long option strings ("--collectonly", "--collect-only") and thus no short alias exists. The lack of a short optstring in that call is the direct reason the parser reports no one-character shortcut (location: src/_pytest/main.py:108-114).
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest --help and inspect the CLI option list; observe that --collect-only appears only in long form.

Attempt to invoke a one-character shortcut, e.g. pytest -C (or other single-letter attempts); observe that the parser reports the option as unrecognized or that the short alias does not trigger collection-only behavior.
Run pytest --collect-only and observe that collect-only behavior works (verifies the long form is functional).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Collect-only mode should be invokable via a short one-character alias in addition to the existing long form(s). The help output should list both the short alias and --collect-only together, and using that short alias should set the same config.option.collectonly attribute so pytest only performs collection (user-visible behavior).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Only the long form(s) (--collectonly, --collect-only) are registered and listed in help; no one-character shortcut is available and attempts to use a single-letter alias are unrecognized.

problem_statement.Title: INTERNALERROR when a failing __repr__ / broken __getattribute__ leads to pytest internal crash
problem_statement.Description: When an object's __repr__ or __getattribute__ raises (for example a bare raise in __repr__), pytest's saferepr code attempts to build a fallback representation but invokes the object again while formatting the fallback. This can raise a second exception during exception-report formatting and results in an INTERNALERROR instead of a safe test failure report.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_try_repr_or_str — incorrect assumption: the fallback may safely format the object. The code's except-block returns '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj) (in the source: return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)), which inserts obj into the format call and therefore invokes obj.__format__/str(obj)/__str__. If the instance has a broken __str__/__repr__/__format__ or a broken __getattribute__, this will execute user code and can raise while pytest is already handling an exception.

2) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_format_repr_exception — incorrect assumption: accessing obj.__class__.__name__ is side-effect-free. The code constructs the fallback using obj.__class__.__name__ (in the source: ... obj.__class__.__name__ ...), which performs an instance attribute lookup and therefore may invoke a user-defined __getattribute__ or descriptors on the instance, running user code while formatting a fallback.

3) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance — these methods catch exceptions from repr() and call the two helpers above to produce a fallback representation. Because the helpers call back into the object (via formatting the object or accessing obj.__class__.__name__), the saferepr pathway is not robust: an exception raised while formatting the fallback will escape the intended safe repr logic and can escalate a user exception into an INTERNALERROR. All three locations above encode the same root cause pattern (unsafe calls into the instance while formatting a fallback). For the hardcoded-value parts of this defect, safe, side-effect-free expressions are type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) (these obtain the object's class name and identity without invoking instance-level attribute lookup).
StepsToReproduce: Create a file with the following code:
class SomeClass:
def __getattribute__(self, attr):
raise
def __repr__(self):
raise
def test():
SomeClass().attr
Run pytest on that file (e.g. pytest -q).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest reports the original user exception as a test failure and uses a non-crashing, user-code-free fallback for object representations when repr() fails (a visible fallback based on the object's type name and id or similarly safe string). No INTERNALERROR should be raised while formatting the traceback.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises an INTERNALERROR during test collection/reporting. The traceback shows a secondary exception (for example RuntimeError: 'No active exception to reraise') originating from the user's __repr__ or attribute access while pytest was building a representation of the object, causing pytest's exception-reporting to abort and print an INTERNALERROR block instead of a normal test failure report.

problem_statement.Title: Traceback paths shown relative to changed cwd when fixture calls os.chdir (should be relative to invocation dir)
problem_statement.Description: When a test or fixture changes the process current working directory (os.chdir / monkeypatch.chdir), pytest formats traceback file paths relative to that new working directory. This makes the displayed path point outside the project root (e.g. "../test_path_error.py:29") and prevents editors/IDEs from resolving the file location. The path shortening for traceback entries is being performed relative to the runtime current working directory rather than the pytest invocation directory (the directory pytest was started from).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_code/code.py::FormattedExcinfo._makepath (the traceback formatter) uses py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) as the base for shortening file paths when abspath is False. That call uses py.path.local() (the process current working directory) as the base for bestrelpath; as a result, when tests change CWD the shortened path is computed relative to that changed directory rather than the invocation directory. (Code: "np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)")

2) src/_pytest/nodes.py::Node._repr_failure_py calls excinfo.getrepr(...) to format test failure tracebacks but does not provide a stable base path. The Node object (via self.session) has access to a stable invocation directory (self.session.startdir, which is set from config.invocation_dir), but that base is not supplied to the exception formatter, so the formatter falls back to using the process CWD.

3) src/_pytest/config/__init__.py::Config.notify_exception similarly calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without supplying the invocation directory (self.invocation_dir) as a base. Because Config has invocation_dir available, not providing it results in internal error reports being shortened relative to the current CWD.

4) The same caller-pattern is present in other places that format exceptions and have access to config/session but do not pass a base path to the formatter:
   - src/_pytest/python.py: the module importer/collector exception paths call getrepr(...) (e.g. when raising CollectError on SyntaxError or ImportError) without passing the invocation directory, despite having self.config available.
   - src/_pytest/terminal.py: pytest_keyboard_interrupt stores excinfo.getrepr(funcargs=True) without passing the terminal reporter's config invocation dir.

5) Precise relationship preserved from the investigation: the path-shortening base used for display is currently py.path.local() (the current working directory). For stable, editor-resolvable display the base should be the pytest invocation directory (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir). In other words: currently the code performs
   py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)
   but the display logic must use the invocation directory as the base when available, i.e.
   invocation_dir.bestrelpath(path)
   (session.startdir is the runtime Session attribute for the invocation directory). If callers have no invocation directory available (early bootstrapping errors), the previous behavior can be used as a fallback.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file test_path_error.py with a fixture that creates a subdirectory and chdirs into it (os.chdir or monkeypatch.chdir).
In the test, raise an assertion failure (e.g. assert False).
Run pytest from the project root (the invocation directory).

Observe the failure report: the traceback file path is shown relative to the new working directory (e.g. "../test_path_error.py:29") rather than relative to the invocation directory ("test_path_error.py:29").
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a test fails, file paths in tracebacks are displayed relative to the pytest invocation directory (the directory pytest was started from) or absolute, so editors/IDEs can resolve the file location consistently regardless of tests changing the process current working directory.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: If a fixture or test changes the current working directory, pytest displays traceback file paths shortened relative to that changed working directory (e.g. "../test_path_error.py:29"), making the displayed path wrong relative to the invocation directory and preventing editors from jumping to the file.

problem_statement.Title: String-condition caching ignores module context: skipif/xfail evaluations cached by expr only
problem_statement.Description: pytest caches the result of evaluating string conditions (e.g. @pytest.mark.skipif("skip")) using only the expression text as the cache key. Because the result depends on the evaluation globals (module-level variables), the cache can return a result computed in one module for the same expression used in another module, causing incorrect skip/xfail outcomes.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) cached_eval uses the expression text as the sole cache key — location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, function cached_eval (lines 21–31). Current behavior: the code reads/writes evalcache[expr] (evalcache = config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default); return evalcache[expr]; evalcache[expr] = eval(...)). Incorrect assumption: the boolean result depends only on the expression string.

2) MarkEvaluator constructs a fresh globals dict for each evaluation and passes it to cached_eval — location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, class MarkEvaluator, methods _getglobals and _istrue (lines 76–80 and 99–101). Observation: _getglobals builds a new dict and, when the test item has an object, updates it with the module's __globals__, which contains stable module identifiers such as "__file__" and "__name__". Caching keyed only by the expression text therefore allows reuse of a result computed under a different module's globals.

3) The eval cache is stored on the Config instance (config._store) under a module-level StoreKey — location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, evalcache_key (line 18) and its use of config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default) (line 23). Effect: cached entries are shared for the entire test session (same Config). This is why an evaluation performed for one module can be reused for another module in the same pytest run.

4) Correct relationship and stable discriminators (diagnosis of the wrong hardcoded key): because evaluation depends on the module-global context, the cache key must include a stable module identity in addition to the expression text. A concrete discriminator available in the merged globals dict is the module filename and module name (d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__")). For example, a cache key of the form (expr, (d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__"))) will separate identical expressions evaluated in different modules. Note: if __file__ and __name__ are absent, their values will be None and the cache entry will be shared under the (None, None) context (this reproduces previous behavior for truly global/no-module contexts).
StepsToReproduce: Run: pytest test_module_1.py test_module_2.py
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: test_should_skip is reported as skipped; test_should_not_skip is executed (not skipped).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Both tests are reported as skipped (the cached result from the first evaluation is reused for the second module).

hints_text:
> I think the most appropriate fix is to simply remove the caching, which I don't think is necessary really, and inline cached_eval into MarkEvaluator._istrue.

I agree:

* While it might have some performance impact with very large test suites which use marks with eval, the simple workaround is to not use the eval feature on those, which is more predictable anyway.
* I don't see a clean way to turn "globals" in some kind of cache key without having some performance impact and/or adverse effects.

So 👍 from me to simply removing this caching. 
As globals are dynamic, i would propose to drop the cache as well, we should investigate reinstating a cache later on

problem_statement.Title: pytest.mark.skip location reporting broken when --runxfail is used
problem_statement.Description: Tests skipped via @pytest.mark.skip / skipif show the skip location as pytest's internal skipping.py (the place where pytest.skip() is called) when pytest is run with --runxfail; without --runxfail the skip location correctly points to the test item.
problem_statement.RootCause:
In src/_pytest/skipping.py, the pytest_runtest_makereport hookwrapper uses a single if/elif/... chain that contains an early branch checking `item.config.option.runxfail`. Concretely, the branch `elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass` (skipping.py lines 273–274) is evaluated before the later branches that handle xfail and the final branch which rewrites skip report locations. Because the runxfail check is an `elif` in the same chain, when `--runxfail` is set that branch is taken and the subsequent branch that performs the skip-location rewrite is never reached.

The location-rewriting branch that does not run under `--runxfail` is the following condition and rewrite (skipping.py lines 294–305):
- condition: `item._store.get(skipped_by_mark_key, True) and rep.skipped and type(rep.longrepr) is tuple`
- rewrite: `_, _, reason = rep.longrepr; filename, line = item.reportinfo()[:2]; rep.longrepr = str(filename), line + 1, reason`

This short-circuited control-flow (an overbroad `runxfail` guard placed as an `elif` in the chain) causes the report's longrepr to remain the exception-origin location (where pytest.skip() was raised inside skipping.py) instead of being rewritten to the test item's file/line. The single location that encodes the defect is src/_pytest/skipping.py in the pytest_runtest_makereport hook (the `elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass` branch and the surrounding xfail/skip handling branches).
StepsToReproduce:
Run pytest with the normal skip status output: pytest -rs
  -> observe: SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip

Run pytest with --runxfail: pytest -rs --runxfail
  -> observe: SKIPPED [1] src/_pytest/skipping.py:238: unconditional skip (location points into pytest internals instead of test file)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a test is skipped by @pytest.mark.skip / skipif the test-reported skip location should always point to the test item (file and line of the test), e.g. 'SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip', regardless of whether pytest was invoked with --runxfail.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When pytest is run with --runxfail, the skip report shows the location of pytest.skip() inside pytest's internals (src/_pytest/skipping.py) instead of the test file/line. Without --runxfail the location is correct.

problem_statement.Title: Regression: dynamically-added xfail (during test body) is ignored in pytest 6.x; failure reported as normal failure
problem_statement.Description: If a test adds an xfail marker to its own node during test execution (for example, calling request.node.add_marker(pytest.mark.xfail(...)) inside the test body), pytest 6.x reports a failing assertion as a normal failure instead of an xfailed test. In pytest 5.x the same pattern resulted in an XFAIL outcome.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py) reads xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) but does not call evaluate_xfail_marks(item) when the stored value is None. Because evaluation is not performed at report time, xfail markers added after the pre-call evaluation (i.e., markers appended during the test body via request.node.add_marker or request.applymarker) are not observed and therefore do not influence the final TestReport.

2) The control-flow/assumption encoded across hooks is as follows: pytest_runtest_setup sets item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item) during setup; pytest_runtest_call re-evaluates evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if item._store[xfailed_key] is None, but this re-evaluation happens before the test body runs (it is done pre-yield in the hookwrapper). pytest_runtest_makereport lacked a final evaluation of evaluate_xfail_marks(item) as a last chance to detect markers added during the call, so dynamically added 'xfail' markers in the test body are ignored when building the report. This missing final evaluation is the regression.

Locations encoding the incorrect assumption (each given equal weight):
- src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport — reads item._store[xfailed_key] but does not re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if the stored value is None, so markers added during the call are not taken into account at report construction.
- src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_setup — evaluates xfail during setup and stores it in item._store[xfailed_key] (this is correct for markers added before test body execution).
- src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_call — re-evaluates evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if item._store[xfailed_key] is None, but that re-evaluation occurs before the test body runs; it therefore cannot detect markers added during the test body.

3) The relationship that must hold: when creating the final TestReport, xfailed must reflect evaluate_xfail_marks(item) executed at report time if the stored value is None, because item.iter_markers(name="xfail") will include markers appended to item.own_markers during the test body. The stored item._store[xfailed_key] should therefore be computed or re-checked at makereport time in cases where it is not already set to a non-None value.
StepsToReproduce: Create test_foo.py with contents:
import pytest
def test_xfail_test(request):
mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason="xfail")
request.node.add_marker(mark)
assert 0
Run pytest on the file with pytest 6.x (for example: pytest -rsx test_foo.py).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If an xfail marker is added to the test node during test execution (inside the test body), a subsequent failure in the same test should be reported as XFAIL in the final test report (i.e., treated like a test marked xfail).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: With pytest 6.x, when a test adds an xfail marker during the test body and then fails, the failure is reported as a normal failure (F) rather than XFAIL. In pytest 5.x the same test was reported as XFAIL.

problem_statement.Title: tmpdir creation fails when getpass.getuser() returns a name containing path-separator characters (e.g. domain\\user)
problem_statement.Description: pytest tmpdir/tmpdir_factory/tmp_path_factory use getpass.getuser() (via get_user()) to build the basetemp path (prefix 'pytest-of-{user}'). If the returned username contains characters treated as path separators on the platform (for example 'contoso\\john_doe' on Windows), constructing and creating the basetemp directory can fail and tmpdir creation raises FileNotFoundError.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:get_user()
   - Incorrect assumption: getpass.getuser() returns a username that is safe to use verbatim as a single filesystem path component.
   - Evidence in code: get_user() currently returns the raw getpass.getuser() value (src/_pytest/tmpdir.py lines 151-159).
   - Effect: an unsanitized username can contain path-separator characters (for example a backslash on Windows) which can be interpreted as directory separators when used in a path component.
   - Correct relationship/value (diagnostic): the username used in filesystem paths must be represented as a single filename component without path separators or other unsafe characters. A concrete safe representation discovered during investigation is: replace any character not in the safe set [A-Za-z0-9._-] with an underscore (regex: r"[^A-Za-z0-9._-]+" -> "_") so e.g. 'contoso\\john_doe' -> 'contoso_john_doe'.

2) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:TempPathFactory.getbasetemp()
   - Incorrect assumption: rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") will always be a single-level directory (no intermediate components), so calling rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) is sufficient.
   - Evidence in code: getbasetemp() constructs rootdir from temproot and the raw user value and calls rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) (src/_pytest/tmpdir.py lines 112-118).
   - Effect: when the user value contains a path separator the resulting Path can represent nested directories (e.g. temproot / 'pytest-of-contoso' / 'john_doe'), and mkdir(..., exist_ok=True) without parents=True fails with FileNotFoundError because intermediate parents do not exist. The make_numbered_dir call that follows assumes the root already exists and will fail if it does not (see src/_pytest/pathlib.py make_numbered_dir which calls new_path.mkdir()).

Notes/Context:
- The repository already applies sanitization to other inputs (for example _mk_tmp sanitizes test node names with re.sub(r"[\W]", "_") in src/_pytest/tmpdir.py), indicating sanitization is an established pattern in this area. However, get_user() is not sanitized in the current code.
StepsToReproduce: On Windows (example): log in via an SSH session that causes getpass.getuser() to return a domain-qualified username (e.g. 'contoso\\john_doe'), then in pytest call tmpdir_factory.mktemp('foobar') or run a test that uses the tmpdir fixture.
Or reproduce locally in cmd.exe: create a file test_tmp.py containing 'def test_tmpdir(tmpdir):\n    pass', then run: set LOGNAME=contoso\\john_doe  && py.test test_tmp.py
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: pytest should create the temporary directory successfully. The basetemp path created under the system temp dir should be a valid filesystem path (e.g. .../Temp/pytest-of-contoso_john_doe/pytest-<n>) and tmpdir/tmp_path fixtures should not raise exceptions when the OS username contains domain separators or other unsafe characters.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Attempting to create a tmpdir raises FileNotFoundError when getpass.getuser() contains path-separator characters. Example error seen on Windows: "FileNotFoundError: [WinError 3] The system cannot find the path specified: 'C:\\Users\\john_doe\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\pytest-of-contoso\\john_doe'" (trace shows pathlib.Path.mkdir failing when creating the basetemp directory).

problem_statement.Title: Confusing/incorrect guidance when pytest.skip() is used at module import-time (missing mention of allow_module_level or skip_module)
problem_statement.Description: When pytest.skip(...) is called during module import (module-level), pytest raises a CollectError with a message that discourages module-level skipping and points to pytestmark, but the message does not mention the existing allow_module_level option (skip(..., allow_module_level=True)) nor the existence of other module-level skip paths. This makes correct usage unclear: the code already supports module-level skipping when allow_module_level=True (and importorskip uses that), but the message suggests module-level skip is not allowed.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Misleading error message location and content (src/_pytest/python.py, Module._importtestmodule): Module._importtestmodule catches skip.Exception (the Skipped outcome) and, when e.allow_module_level is False, raises a CollectError whose message begins "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." and points users to pytestmark. That message omits any mention of the allow_module_level flag on the Skipped exception or of any convenience helper; as a result it discourages a supported escape hatch.

2) The outcomes API already defines an explicit escape hatch for module-level skipping (src/_pytest/outcomes.py): the Skipped exception and the skip() function accept an allow_module_level boolean parameter (default allow_module_level=False). The default encodes a conservative assumption (module-level skip is disallowed unless explicitly opted-in), but the error message in Module._importtestmodule does not surface this existing option. (See Skipped.__init__(..., allow_module_level: bool = False) and skip(msg, *, allow_module_level: bool = False).)

3) Another existing API mirrors module-level skip semantics (src/_pytest/outcomes.py, importorskip): importorskip raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) to indicate module-level skip on failed import. This shows that module-level skipping is intentionally supported in at least one public helper, yet the CollectError message discouraging pytest.skip at module-level does not make that relationship clear.

4) There is no ergonomics-focused convenience helper named skip_module in the codebase (search: no skip_module symbol defined). The combination of the discouraging message and absence of a clearly-named helper reduces discoverability of the allow_module_level escape-hatch.
StepsToReproduce: Create a module using Python 3.8-only syntax (e.g., `def foo(a, /, b): return a + b`) and a test file that imports that module.
Run pytest with an older Python interpreter (e.g., 3.6 or 3.7) and attempt to skip the module by calling pytest.skip("Requires Python >= 3.8") at module top-level (no allow_module_level).

Observe that pytest reports a CollectError and prints a message starting: "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." suggesting pytestmark usage but not mentioning `allow_module_level=True` nor any convenience helper.

Contrast that with calling pytest.skip("Requires Python >= 3.8", allow_module_level=True) during module import: that results in a module-level skip and no CollectError. Also note pytest.importorskip(...) raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) when import fails, demonstrating the code supports module-level skipping.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible guidance should accurately reflect the library's supported API: when pytest.skip is invoked at module import-time the guidance should explain that module-level skipping is possible via pytest.skip(<reason>, allow_module_level=True) (and that importorskip already uses allow_module_level=True), while still recommending marks for skipping individual tests when appropriate. The message should not imply module-level skip is categorically unsupported when the code provides an explicit opt-in flag.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pytest raises a CollectError with the message that begins "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." and directs users to use pytest.mark or pytestmark for module skipping, without mentioning the existing allow_module_level parameter on pytest.skip or any convenience helper. This leads users to believe module-level skipping is unsupported even though code paths like importorskip use allow_module_level=True to skip modules.

Hi @omry,

Thanks for raising this.

Definitely we should improve that message. 

> Explain skip with allow_module_level in the error message. this seems in conflict with the spirit of the message.

I'm 👍 on this. 2 is also good, but because `allow_module_level` already exists and is part of the public API, I don't think introducing a new API will really help, better to improve the docs of what we already have.

Perhaps improve the message to something like this:

```
Using pytest.skip outside of a test will skip the entire module, if that's your intention pass `allow_module_level=True`. 
If you want to skip a specific test or entire class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.
```

I think we can drop the `pytestmark` remark from there, it is not skip-specific and passing `allow_module_level` already accomplishes the same.

Thanks @nicoddemus.

> Using pytest.skip outside of a test will skip the entire module, if that's your intention pass `allow_module_level=True`. 
If you want to skip a specific test or entire class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.

This sounds clearer.
Can you give a bit of context of why the message is there in the first place?
It sounds like we should be able to automatically detect if this is skipping a test or skipping the entire module (based on the fact that we can issue the warning).

Maybe this is addressing some past confusion, or we want to push people toward `pytest.mark.skip[if]`, but if we can detect it automatically - we can also deprecate allow_module_level and make `skip()` do the right thing based on the context it's used in.
> Maybe this is addressing some past confusion

That's exactly it, people would use `@pytest.skip` instead of `@pytest.mark.skip` and skip the whole module:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/2338#issuecomment-290324255

For that reason we don't really want to automatically detect things, but want users to explicitly pass that flag which proves they are not doing it by accident.

Original issue: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/607
Having looked at the links, I think the alternative API to skip a module is more appealing.
Here is a proposed end state:

1. pytest.skip_module is introduced, can be used to skip a module.
2. pytest.skip() is only legal inside of a test. If called outside of a test, an error message is issues.
Example:

> pytest.skip should only be used inside tests. To skip a module use pytest.skip_module. To completely skip a test function or a test class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.

Getting to this end state would include deprecating allow_module_level first, directing people using pytest.skip(allow_module_level=True) to use pytest.skip_module().

I am also fine with just changing the message as you initially proposed but I feel this proposal will result in an healthier state.

This occurs on Python 3.9 (observed in the report) and results in an extra '@t' line appearing between the failing assert line and the assertion details/diff.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_code/source.py:getstatementrange_ast computes a statement slice by calling get_statement_startend2 to get candidate start/end indices, possibly expanding the range with BlockFinder/tokenize, and then trimming trailing blank/comment lines only. It does not trim trailing decorator lines (lines beginning with '@'). Because decorated FunctionDef/ClassDef AST line-number semantics changed in newer CPython (the lineno attributed to the node and/or decorators can move), the computed end index can end up such that the returned slice includes a decorator line that belongs to the next statement. The code that trims at the end only checks for comments/blank lines and therefore allows decorator lines to be returned and printed.

2) src/_pytest/_code/source.py:get_statement_startend2 converts AST lineno values into 0-based indices using explicit numeric arithmetic (x.lineno - 1) and, for 'finalbody'/'orelse', uses val[0].lineno - 1 - 1 (i.e. subtracting 2). These hardcoded offsets encode assumptions about how AST nodes advertise their lineno and about orelse/finally boundaries. Those adjustments are sensitive to AST lineno semantics across Python versions and therefore participate in the symptom where the computed end can reach decorator lines.

3) src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py contains a special-case that sets lineno = item.decorator_list[0].lineno for decorated FunctionDef items when inserting imports (i.e. rewrite.py already treats decorator-list lineno semantics specially elsewhere). This shows that decorator/lineno behavior has been handled inconsistently across the codebase and that special-casing exists in other locations.
StepsToReproduce: Create file test_statement.py with the following contents:
from pytest import fixture
def t(foo):
return foo
@fixture
def foo():
return 1
def test_right_statement(foo):
assert foo == (3 + 2) * (6 + 9)
@t
def inner():
return 2
assert 2 == inner
@t
def outer():
return 2

Run pytest with Python 3.9 (pytest 6.2.5 used in the report). Observe the failure output; note that a '@t' decorator line from the subsequent inner function is printed between the assertion line and the assertion failure details.
Repeat the same test with Python 3.7 to confirm the decorator line is not printed in that environment.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an assertion fails, pytest displays only the source lines belonging to the failing statement (and nearby context that legitimately belongs to that statement). It must not include decorator lines that belong to a subsequently-defined function or class.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On Python 3.9, the displayed snippet for a failing assertion can include one or more decorator lines (e.g. '@t') from the next function defined after the assertion. The decorator appears between the assertion source line and the assertion failure/diff, causing confusing output.
